data.title: Aflac Gobbles Up New Business While Also Driving Strong Margins Among Insurance Peers
data.description:
Today's Stock Pick: An Insurance Brand That Keeps Quacking

Lately, I've been talking a lot about the role of risk management in analyzing stocks, so today's article focuses on a sector whose business is literally risk mitigation.

The insurance industry is known for underwriting risk, and a company like Aflac (AFL) with its iconic duck branding has been on my radar for a while, having gone up around +3% since I called it a buy in my Nov. 2024 article.

Some factors that drove my bullish view included insurance policy growth and retention, so this time around I'll be running this stock through an updated analysis with the latest data, well ahead of their next earnings results scheduled in a few weeks on Feb. 4th.

According to its profile on Seeking Alpha, Aflac has actually been around since the 1950s and "provides supplemental health and life insurance products."

Here is a snippet of what risk they insure, from their company website:

Read on to learn what changed since my 2024 outlook, and why you should care as an investor.

The Thesis: There is Enough Reason To Reaffirm My Buy Rating From 2024 Again

In my followup coverage on Aflac today, I'm reaffirming my prior buy rating and my thesis argues that several upside factors will continue driving strength for this stock, overwhelming the downside factors.

In particular, strong profit margins within its peer group, strong A-level credit ratings, and organic business growth / insurance sales were among the top scoring factors in my review.

The following worksheet shows how the stock did across 8 weighted categories:

Read on as the next several sections go into more detail on these topics, but also why they don't all carry the same weight.

Price & Technicals: No Clear Buy Or Sell Signal Right Now

In my last coverage of Aflac, I didn't talk much about technical chart patterns, so this time I am including some discussion on that, to add some additional color to the discussion. Consider the technical chart I made below in ycharts:

In this chart we see a sell signal last summer (red circle / death cross) and this fall a buy signal (green circle / golden cross). Since then, the 50-day and 200-day SMA have been running mostly parallel to each other.

Without a clear buy or sell signal, I would call this a neutral pattern, justifying a hold. However, keep reading to see if my fundamental analysis changes this purely technical view.

Macro Demand: Multiple Potential Tailwinds To Consider In The Insurance Sector

When it comes to macroeconomic demand drivers, we already know some of the insurance products that Aflac deals with, so I think upside could come from further momentum in those.

One driver of upside could be further demand for pet insurance, for instance. Last year, an article by the American Veterinary Medical Foundation highlighted the steady momentum growth in this niche in recent years:

Aflac could continue to see further upside in that niche if this trend continues.

Further, let's not ignore the role that dental insurance also plays in healthcare and patient needs, transferring some of that risk to an insurer.

For instance, the research firm Research And Markets was cited in another major financial website last autumn with an optimistic outlook of this niche:

The global dental insurance market is projected to grow from $200 billion in 2024 to $428.32 billion by 2033, driven by a CAGR of 8.83% from 2025 to 2033. Factors such as rising oral health awareness, increasing dental care costs, and expanded employer-sponsored coverage are key growth drivers.

Again, Aflac has a presence in this niche, so market upside could be in its favor. Although not a major health insurer like an Aetna or US Healthcare, the role of supplemental health insurance plays an economic role in passing the risk of certain healthcare costs onto an insurer.

In terms of another huge market, life insurance, I would highlight a December note from rating agency Fitch that had a neutral outlook for 2026. Their note said:

Fitch believes life insurers’ strong balance sheets will mitigate a potential slowdown in economic growth, heightened macroeconomic volatility, and geopolitical uncertainty.

Again, this type of outlook could be in favor for Aflac, who has a life insurance segment too.

Given the multiple points raised in this section, that favor the upside, I am bullish on the macro environment for Aflac going forward.

It will also be a huge business impact, as I think this insurer will try to show it can continue to achieve organic growth in new insurance policies sold.

Company Growth: Signs of Organic Growth in US & Japan Markets Can Drive Upside

Next, let's consider any recent company growth factors.

Although it's been a few months since Aflac's last quarterly results, we can consider some key metrics that point to organic growth, rather than just revenue growth that comes from insurance rate increases.

For instance, the Q3 press release (pg 2) mentioned:

Aflac U.S. sales increased 2.8% in the quarter to $390 million, primarily benefiting from sales of group life and disability products. For the first nine months of the year, total new sales increased 3.0% to $1.0 billion.

I think this YoY top-line growth is indicative of organic momentum in the life insurance segment, and can drive future revenues since those policies can keep generating premiums as long as the policyholder stays with Aflac. It can be called a "sticky" product, unlike selling an apple that generates revenue only that day it is sold.

The firm has also grown in Japan, showing its brand reach beyond just the US, and in particular there seems to be momentum in the niche of cancer insurance.

In that same press release, Aflac Chairman and CEO Daniel P. Amos said:

Looking at our operations in Japan, I am pleased with Aflac Japan’s 11.8% year-over-year sales increase in the quarter and ongoing strong premium persistency. The strong sales were driven largely by continued sales of our cancer insurance product Miraito, which was launched in March.

This favorable commentary comes in the wake of highly impressive YoY top-line growth (Q3 results, pg. 4):

In the upcoming earnings results, I think analysts will be looking for a continuation of this organic growth momentum in the US and Japan, building on top of the existing tailwind. This also positions Aflac well in specialty insurance lines like in the realm of cancer care, adding to its diversified insurance portfolio.

However, like I mentioned in other articles on insurance stocks, although insurance premiums are a key revenue driver it is common for insurers to earn additional income from investments held. In that regard, the same Q3 results were positive, mentioning "net investment gains of $275 million this quarter compared to net investment losses of $1.4 billion in the third quarter of 2024."

So, I am compelled to be highly bullish when it comes to company growth, in the wake of such impressive Q3 results and the future upside that can be gained from new policies written in Q3. Both organic policy growth and investment income are a major business impact to Aflac so this section gets full weight in my overall score.

Profitability: The Highest Margin Among Key Peers

In this section, let's talk about some margins and also what could impact earnings at this insurance business.

First, here is a comparison of net income margins between Aflac and 3 peers I picked as they are not property/casualty insurers but focus on the spectrum of life insurance and healthcare supplemental just like Aflac does: Metlife (MET), Prudential (PRU), and Cigna (CI):

In this 3-year period studied, you will notice that Aflac not only has the highest profit margin (23.55%) but that it has consistently been well above these peers in this period shown, a sign of earnings resiliency.

If that trend continues, it should give added confidence to investors and analysts that Aflac is more efficient at turning a profit that many peers.

Second, in the insurance sector a key impact to earnings can come from spikes in claims. We've seen that in some of my articles covering property/casualty(P&C) insurers who were faced with the California wildfires as well as catastrophic weather events some time ago. However, this niche being non-P&C it is not exposed to the same types of risk but rather health and mortality events.

To smooth out that picture, I think a key insurance metric called the "benefit ratio" is a good one to look at:

In the above table from the Q3 deck (pg 11), we see that in its home market of the US, Aflac has been able to maintain a benefit ratio under 52%. A low ratio is better in this business, so in this case only around half of premiums are going towards benefit payouts, while half goes towards business expenses and profits.

If they can maintain close to that trend again in upcoming earnings results, and the rest of 2026, that ought to support investor confidence, and because they are not insuring property there is no major spike in quarterly benefit claims in the event of a major storm or wildfire.

Consider from income statement data that the cost of policy benefits did not see any major spikes in a single quarter in the last few years, and had a relatively tight range:

Therefore, based on the evidence in this section, I am highly bullish about the profitability potential of this insurer. It also seems the consensus has a similar view, with 13 upwards revisions to earnings estimates vs 0 downward.

Competitive Position: A Unique Branding Story in A Competitive Sector

Now, let's consider the competitive positioning of Aflac within the peer group I suggested.

Based on public market data, Aflac has a market cap of around $60B, lower than or close to peers like Cigna (CI) who has a market cap of around $64.5B, and Metlife (MET) whose market cap is around $60B. Its peer Prudential (PRU) is slightly smaller at around $43.5B.

If considering only market cap alone, Aflac is not the biggest in this selected peer group, with Cigna being much larger and also encompassing more than just supplemental health benefits.

My opinion is that Aflac differentiates itself in this group with its unique branding and some of us grew up with its duck commercials on TV. In fact, I fondly remember those TV ads many years ago.

Back in 2020, CNN Business even wrote about the success of the company's unique branding:

Aflac CEO Dan Amos, who’s been the head of the company since 1990, told CNN Business he’s pleasantly surprised by just how much the duck resonates with consumers. The brand awareness that the ad created is a big reason for the company’s success today, he said.

'Within three years of the first ad, our sales in the US doubled and our name recognition went from under 10% to around 90%,' Amos said.

Further, I think if you combine the longstanding brand penetration together with the diversified portfolio of insurance products (life, dental, vision, pet, etc.) it certainly can differentiate itself in this field despite not having the largest market cap.

Just last year, Aflac announced that it is the 19th year in a row it has been named among the world's most ethical companies by Ethisphere, and "is the only insurance company in the world to appear on this list since the award's inception in 2007."

So, my opinion is bullish in terms of this company's competitive positioning. Its positioning also has a huge business impact, I think, because of the need to attract consumers who are shopping for the best insurance rates but also good service and claims processing.

Balance Sheet Risk: Among The Top Credit Ratings

Balance sheet risk is an important topic to all businesses but especially for insurers because they are highly regulated and have to be able to pay out insurance claims, so the money has to come from somewhere.

Notable to mention is that Aflac has among the strongest "A" level credit ratings from major agencies like S&P and Moody's, according to its' company website:

Such strong credit ratings could help it get the most favorable financing terms now and in the future, compared to firms with much worse credit ratings.

Second, let's talk about debt vs equity:

In this peer comparison, we see that Aflac has the lowest D/E in this peer group, at just 0.30, but also we see that this peer group overall has generally low leverage risk (D/E below 1.0) compared to some sectors I've covered.

This is another reason why it's important to compare to similar businesses in the same sector, rather than other sectors like REITs who may use heavy leverage to grow their property portfolios.

Third, it is worth mentioning also that balance sheet data from Seeking Alpha shows that Aflac is heavily exposed to debt securities, to the tune of over +$84B, and in fact it is their biggest asset exposure:

Unlike cash which does not necessarily have market risk and is the most liquid of assets, debt securities do have market risk because they can be traded actively and hence their values can fluctuate, meaning that the firm may have to sell such assets at a loss sometimes.

Fluctuating asset values also impact book value. Such assets also have interest rate risk as well since they are typically generating interest income for the firm.

We can see from the income statement, for example, that in the quarter ending June the firm made just over +$1B in interest income in a single quarter, but that quarter it also saw a net loss of around $420MM on investment losses.

So, it is important for readers to see how all those nuts and bolts fit into the big picture of this firm, and from a risk standpoint too, as there is always the future risk of investment losses on those securities whether on paper or realized.

Nevertheless, the strong credit rating and low D/E are mitigating factors in this story, and so I am highly bullish in this category. Again, this topic has a high business impact for an insurance business especially who is expected to be well capitalized and manage risk effectively.

Dividend Risk: A Low Payout Ratio Is In Focus

When it comes to dividend risk, the two factors I've been watching are payout ratio and proven dividend growth over a longer period.

We can see, for instance, that Aflac has steadily grown its dividend over the 10 year period shown in the chart below, indicating a commitment to return capital to shareholders but also resilience the face of global market shocks like the 2020/2021 pandemic:

Besides dividend resilience, what could impact the dividend going forward is the payout ratio, and the good news is that Seeking Alpha data shows it is only 29.9%. My benchmark is usually 75% or less, so a 30% payout ratio leaves lots of cash wiggle room in the event of a downturn.

What could also add confidence to dividend stability/growth is not only the strong profit margins mentioned earlier as they can drive profits but also a positive EPS consensus, lessening the risk of a dividend cut. This matters to dividend investors since such a cut could negatively impact the stock's share price but also provide lower dividend income.

For the reasons above, I am highly bullish in this category too. However, I gave it slightly less weight / business impact in my overall score as I think it is less impactful than topics like balance sheet risk and top-line growth drivers.

Valuation vs Upside: A Justified Higher Multiple

In this section, let's wind down the discussion today by taking a look at valuation metrics and price forecasts, using my worksheet which uses data from Seeking Alpha's valuation metrics, earnings consensus, and Wall St ratings data:

In this worksheet, we see that the fwd price/earnings multiple and the trailing price/book multiples are highly overvalued vs the sector average.

While at first glance the market paying such a premium on this stock may seem excessive, and that is understandable, one should also consider that the firm's strong profit margins within its peer group (which I showed earlier) and its low benefit payout ratio (showed earlier) also could be driving forces in market confidence. Its heavy focus on supplemental health insurance rather than insuring property such as homes and cars also puts it in a different risk bucket than P&C insurers, which I mentioned earlier.

In terms of price targets, my own target implies a further +25% upside, while the avg. Wall St target implies a lower upside at around +2.3%.

Therefore, I am bullish in this section because the evidence shows a justified premium valuation but also continued future upside forecasts. However, this section weighs less in my overall score, than some other core business factors.

Challenge To My Thesis: Elevated Cyber Risk in the Insurance Sector

A key challenge to my bullish call, not yet discussed, is something that I think can impact this entire sector but also a factor that Aflac has personally dealt with.

Cyber risk is a major threat to insurers due to the vast amount of private/confidential data that insurers have on thousands or millions of their customers, and the increasingly digital nature of insurance.

A major cyber attack where such data is compromised carries huge reputational and brand risk for an insurer, just like it can for a bank.

From the Aflac official site, the company itself disclosed such an incident:

An unauthorized actor obtained personal information from an Aflac system on June 12, 2025. Aflac determined on December 4, 2025 that the files potentially impacted in the security incident likely contained personal information triggering notification under applicable law.

Further, the cyber threat to insurers is global in nature. Last summer, Insurance Business magazine said:

The global insurance industry is facing a growing cyber threat as the notorious hacker collective known as Scattered Spider turns its sights on financial and underwriting firms, following a disruptive spree targeting retailers in recent months.

Therefore, I think investors in major stocks in this sector, including Aflac, should realize that those firms can continue being a target, despite investing in considerable IT security frameworks. The reputational risk of a major data theft of private info could spill into customers leaving, which is bad for future business.

Conclusion: A Dividend Income Idea That Has Shown Resilience Over Time

In conclusion, today I've shown why my prior buy rating should be reaffirmed again. Although the technical chart showed neutral patterns lately, it is the fundamental analysis that really strengthens the bullish case and Aflac especially shines in profit margins, new policy growth in the US and Japan, and an attractive balance sheet profile.

It will also be included in my dividend ideas for this month, due to 10 years of steady dividend growth, so my call to action for readers is to add Aflac to your watchlist of potential buys to research further, and also a dividend idea too.

Upcoming Earnings: What To Look For

Looking ahead, with the next earnings results coming out in less than 2 weeks on Feb. 4th, here are 4 key points I think you should look out for:

  • Expectations are for a +8.6% YoY EPS growth. Aflac already beat EPS estimates in the last two quarterly results, so a meet or beat can reinforce a bullish thesis, while a significant miss could negatively impact the stock. Its strong profit margins so far could provide the tailwind needed.
  • As I said in the article, organic growth levers can come from new business written in the US and Japan, so I will look for further evidence that the insurer is growing new insurance policies and not losing customers. This will bolster the top-line bullish case further as new policies written in Q4 can drive future revenue flows.
  • An improvement to operating cashflow growth (YoY) could boost the stock, as Seeking Alpha data shows it has seen a YoY decline lately, so I think analysts will be looking for clarity in that regard. The importance of cashflow cannot be overlooked as it plays a key role in dividend support and company reinvestment.
  • Lastly, I am looking for additional insight from Aflac on its cancer insurance product Miraito (mentioned in this article), and whether it continued to see organic growth in Q4, as a factor that could contribute to Aflac's market positioning in its peer group but also providing risk coverage for patients in this typically very costly clinical niche area of oncology care.

data.title: 3 Things To Consider Ahead Of The FOMC January 2026 Decision
data.description:
On January 27-28, 2026, the Federal Open Market Committee (“FOMC”) will meet to discuss its monetary policy. Ahead of the decision, the CME FedWatch tool has a 95.6% probability that rates will not change. While the target rate stays at 350-375 basis points, stock markets and the current U.S. government administration may view no rate cuts as defiance.

Stock markets want lower interest rates to justify a lower risk premium. The S&P 500 (SPY) (IVV) (SP500) is up by 1.07%, underperforming markets like South Korea (EWY) and Peru (EPU) year-to-date. Chart readers might notice the bearish “multiple top” on the S&P 500. Although readers cannot rely on the chart indicator, the FOMC is a potential catalyst for the downside risks ahead.

Japan’s (EWJ) 10-year and 40-year treasury bonds are at multi-decade highs. Citadel’s Ken Griffin said that Japan’s troubled bond market is a warning to the U.S. to get its over $38 trillion national debt in order. Still, fiscal policy is beyond the FOMC’s mandate. It sets interest rates to achieve maximum employment at a stable inflation rate of 2.0%.

What will the FOMC say after it likely holds interest rates?

Readers have three considerations.

1) PCE Inflation at 2.8%

In November 2025, the personal consumption expenditure price index showed that both headline and core inflation were 2.8%. This is above the FOMC’s 2.0% target. In October, the Bureau of Economic Analysis (“BEA”) reported a 2.7% figure for both core and headline. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, since they are volatile.

The Fed might assign a lower weighting to those two figures. The federal government shut down during that time, limiting the data’s durability. Fortunately, it had more complete December consumer price index and non-farm payroll reports to review.

Wages increased by 0.1% in October and by 0.3% in November. That helps consumers limit the impact that inflation has on their disposable income. That is showing up in corporate earnings reports. Procter & Gamble (PG) Chief Financial Officer Andre Schulten said that its most recent quarterly report represented the softest quarter of the fiscal year. Still, the global conglomerate offset domestic weakness with strong growth in France, Spain, and Italy. That offset softer demand from Germany.

Seeking Alpha’s Peer tab on PG stock is a source for further stock ideas.

In the monthly chart above, all of the consumer staple stocks are up. Colgate-Palmolive (CL), Reckitt Benckiser (RBGLY), Kimberly-Clark (KMB), Henkel (HENOY), and Church & Dwight (CHD) have positive returns. The quant rankings below show that RBGLY stock has a higher industry and sector rank than PG stock.

2) December CPI

Last month, the CPI increased 0.3% on a seasonally adjusted basis. CPI for all items in the last month increased 2.7%. The index for shelter increased 0.4%. It contributed the most to the monthly increase. Homebuilder companies like D.R. Horton (DHI) started to rally before losing momentum. Lennar (LEN) shares spiked to nearly $125 before they pulled back to $111.84. LEN stock lost 8.52% last week. Irrespective of the Fed’s monetary policy, the treasury bond market is anticipating the U.S. dollar (UUP) (DXY) to weaken. Yields for longer-term bonds like the 7-10-year Treasury bill (IEF) and 20+ year Treasury bill (TLT) continued to trend lower.

Throughout this year, investors aggressively hedged their bets against the currency. They sent gold (GLD) toward $5,000. Silver (SLV) traded at a milestone of $100 an ounce. Despite fears of U.S. aggression toward Greenland easing, watch out for the currency weakening further. Just as investors are bidding non-U.S. indices higher in 2026, they are also potentially creating a bubble in the metals market.

3) December Non-Farm Payroll Data

On January 9, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that employment increased by +50,000 and the unemployment rate of 4.4% changed little in December. However, it revised the NFP for October down by 68,000, from -105,000 to -173,000. It also revised November figures by 8,000, from +64,000 to +56,000. Readers may infer that the Fed would estimate that the BLS will revise December figures down by an average of those two figures, or by around 38,000.

The smaller monthly job decline would sharply decrease the odds of the Fed cutting rates this month. Instead, inflation rates above its target would encourage the FOMC to hold rates. Even though a rate hike is not impossible, it is also not likely to happen. Besides, investors should wait for Fed Chair Jerome Powell to comment on the upward revision in the Q3 2025 Gross Domestic Product.

The BEA reported that GDP in the third quarter topped 4.4%. Despite fears that tariffs would hurt trade, U.S. exports increased, lifting the real GDP reading up by 0.1%.

The waiting game on the legality of tariffs also has an impact on GDP. While exporters wait for the Supreme Court to rule on the matter. In the meantime, the U.S. President threatened to impose a 100% tariff on Canada (EWC). This is in reaction to the country making a trade deal with China.

Investors may take advantage of the tariff noise. Consider buying Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ). After the U.S. removed the Venezuelan leader, markets initially mispriced CNQ stock. It was thought that oil supplies from the country would hurt Canadian exports. But since those are different markets, CNQ shares almost immediately rebounded from a low of around $30 to close at $36 on January 23.

Your Takeaway

At the question-and-answer session following the FOMC statement, expect mainstream media to ask Fed Chair Powell about the Justice Department’s investigation. Powell already issued a public statement at the time. That proactive response should shift the media’s attention back to the rate decision.

Some of the more insightful questions the media might ask the Fed include its view on the US dollar’s slump compared to the rise in silver and gold. The rising national debt is a fiscal matter. However, the Fed might weigh in on the debt’s impact on jobs and inflation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 'The King Is Naked': Uncomfortable Truths About Starbucks' Dividend (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Introduction

Starbucks (SBUX) has gone nowhere since 2022, offering only some opportunities for swing traders who buy at $75 and then sell as the stock approaches $100. China, which was once a real driver of growth, has turned into a bloodbath, so Starbucks announced that it will sell to the Chinese investment fund Boyu Capital 60% of its local retail business. But even its domestic operations have struggled, as U.S. consumers are becoming more cost-conscious and have traded down to cheaper coffee shops. India was considered to be the solution, but it seems that soft spending is an issue there, too. Meanwhile, Starbucks had to face increasingly high input costs, especially due to coffee and sugar, with the additional burden of seeing extra tariffs on key countries for coffee production, such as Brazil, Switzerland (yes, it is the second largest exporter of coffee), and Vietnam. Starbucks changed CEOs in an attempt to pursue a turnaround. It has also committed to dividend hikes, trying to soothe shareholders as the company is trying to figure things out. But this pushed the debt higher, and many investors fear that Starbucks' dividend policy may become unsustainable. This is why I argued several times that I don't think Starbucks represents a compelling turnaround opportunity.

Starbucks is about to report its Q1 2026 earnings. Let's then prepare ourselves for what the company may announce.

Starbucks' Q1 2026 Earnings Report

Starbucks ended its fiscal year 2025 with a decent report, where global growth was 5%, with comparable store sales up by 1%. After bleeding for 7 consecutive quarters, the company was finally able to interrupt this losing streak, which is already a positive sign. However, Starbucks CEO Brian Niccol had to report that the company's operating margin declined by 500 bps to 9.4% because of input cost inflation. Thus, it is no surprise that the company's EPS was down 34% YoY to $0.52. Moreover, U.S. comparable sales were flat YoY: tickets increased by 1%, but transactions were down 1% YoY.

During the earnings call, SBUX CFO Cathy Smith admitted that "coffee prices have not retreated". Moreover, the forecast is that coffee will remain at elevated prices through 2026, perhaps coming down a bit in the second semester of this year.

Margin compression is thus guaranteed, even when the YoY comparison seems easy. This goes along with a $500M investment in labor that Starbucks has announced through its "Green Apron Service", which aims at increasing labor hours and streamlining internal operations, while revitalizing Starbucks' culture of hospitality to help it rebuild a strong relationship with its guests.

In this situation, Starbucks has decided to give only directional guidance, without releasing any financial targets. We will have to wait until the day after its Q1 2026 earnings call, when the company will hold its Investor Day. That is the key event to follow to understand what numbers Starbucks has in mind and to assess whether they are realistic or not. Until then, I think the stock is somewhat muted.

The setup that is forming for the upcoming earnings report seems to me as follows.

  1. On the one hand, we will have to see whether transactions and traffic are growing, not only internationally, but also domestically
  2. We should not expect the company's margins to increase, as they will feel both the weight of coffee inflation and that of the company's recent investments in labor.

The best outlook I can think of for 2026 is that it could be a rebuild year. If Starbucks is able to at least stabilize its results for a few quarters, investors could start believing that a new base for future growth could be forming. The issue would then be to determine the appropriate growth rate. Right now, the debate is still on whether Starbucks can sustain continued revenue growth or if its sales keep on struggling. We saw China reporting stronger growth. However, it came with a price since Starbucks invested big in promotions and discounting to gain market share. While this is a move that aims at attracting more customers, it is not written anywhere that this new traffic converts to a higher average ticket. In fact, it could be dilutive to this extent, since customers come to purchase discounted items or to benefit from promotions.

This is why I believe that Starbucks will likely report revenues of $9.5B, which would represent a 1% increase YoY. When it comes to forecasting the company's EPS, given that we have no guidance issued by the company, I believe that we are still in deeply negative territory. With coffee prices still very high and higher labor expenses, I think a 15% EPS drop is possible. This would mean that Starbucks could report EPS of $0.58.

Counterpoints

This article issues a sell rating, and I would like to consider some counterarguments to this. The foundation is where I talked about 2026 being a year of rebuilding. If Starbucks is able to achieve traffic inflection, especially in the U.S., not only for a quarter, but for several quarters in a row, the narrative around its earnings could change substantially. This would be even more true if Starbucks achieved this while improving its internal efficiency and reducing promotions. We would have a powerful combination of increasing traffic and expanding margins, which would trigger a positive re-rating. There is also another catalyst. The day after the company reports its earnings, we will hear what its executives will say at the Investor Day. Until then, Starbucks has suspended its financial guidance. Once we hear the targets the company commits to and we can see whether the dividend is sustainable or not, things could become clearer and investors could feel safer in betting on Starbucks' turnaround. I think no tailwind can come from coffee prices, at least for a year. I also don't think China could play a positive role in the turnaround. But there is a chance that positive domestic news and convincing financial targets could renew investors' confidence in this company.

Valuation

Is Starbucks a buy right now? I don't think so. The company trades at a FWD P/E of 41, which is the outcome of analysts lowering their EPS estimates. Its FWD EV/EBITDA is 23 and its P/FCF is 23. Starbucks is currently more expensive than Big Tech, with the difference that it is not a defensive stock anymore and that it lacks the strong growth prospects that many stocks with these multiples usually present. Let's now look at its dividend. The FWD yield is 2.6%, but its payout ratio has crossed a dangerous threshold as it now stands at 115%. The truth is better spotted when we look at the cash flow statement. In the last twelve months, Starbucks generated $2.4B in FCF, but sent to its shareholders $2.7B via dividends. We are not at a sustainable level, and we will either see Starbucks quickly turnaround and increase its FCF, or I fear that a dividend cut will be announced soon.

If a dividend cut happens, the stock would lose further traction, and I could see a possible downfall to a price of $55-$60. Right now, the stock already prices in that a turnaround will materialize, and it also seems that investors still have in mind that Starbucks deserves a premium valuation because it is a high-quality company. I believe the numbers say something different. As a result, I am sticking to my sell rating as the stock heads into earnings.

data.title: S&P 500: 2 Reasons For Accelerated Rotation To Value
data.description:
Value stocks started outperforming the SP500

My investment analysis for this article is twofold. First, I want to review recent market data to argue that a rotation from growth to value is underway. And second, I want to identify the top reasons that can cause the rotation to continue or even accelerate in the near future.

Let me start with the first part of my argument. The piece is quite straightforward in my view: after lagging the SP500 for years, value stocks have begun to outperform lately. As an example, the chart below compares the price performance of the SPY as a proxy for the SP500 index against two popular funds with a strong value flavor: Vanguard S&P 500 Value Index Fund ETF Shares (VOOV) and the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). SPY has been leading both funds by a large gap in the past 10-year, 5-year, and also 3-year timeframes. But recent performance shows a reversal favoring value-oriented funds. For example, in the last month, SCHD gained 5.81 percent and VOOV gained 1.45 percent, while SPY struggled with volatility and stayed essentially flat (with a 0.18 percent gain). This trend is similar in the past 3 months too and is more pronounced in terms of total returns, as both VOOV and VYM pay higher yields than SPY.

Fund flow data in focus

Investors always vote with their feet—or - or money, and the latest fund flow data clearly show this. As an example, the next 3 charts show the fund flow data in the past month for SPY, VOOV, and also SCHD. As seen, SPY ETF has suffered A) a net negative flow over this period of -$4.6B, and also B) far more trading days with negative fund flows than days with positive flows. In contrast, VOOV has only experienced net positive flows in the past month. Admittedly, being a relatively smaller fund (with an AUM of around $6B, vs. about $700 billion for SPY), the data can be sporadic. This is where SCHD came to help out. With a far larger AUM of around $76 billion, the trading data for SCHD offers much better statistical significance. As seen, the fund flow pattern for SCHD is exactly the opposite of that for SPY in that A) it has attracted a net positive fund flow over the past month of $565M, and also B) it also experienced far more trading days with positive fund flows than days with negative flows.

With this background, I will next identify the top reasons that can cause investors' rotation to value stocks/funds to continue or even accelerate going forward.

The rotation has more room to go

The first reason on my list is quite simple: the value gap between value stocks and the S&P 500 is still above the historical average and thus has more room to normalize. As an estimate of their valuation gaps, the next two charts show the dividend yield spread (YS) between VOOV, SCHD, and SPY in the past decade, rightly a business cycle in my experience. As seen, the YS has hovered above their long-term average most of the time since early 2020. The YS for both VOOV and SCHD has peaked recently at around 0.9% for VOOV and 2.8% for SCHD, respectively. However, the YS for both funds has started narrowing quite quickly in my view recently. Yet, the current YS is still above the long-term average for both funds, which leads me to see more room for narrowing.

Other risks and final thoughts

The second reason on my list that can support a continuation or acceleration rotation involves my interest outlook and also the role of AI deployment. In a nutshell, my outlook leads me to see the benefits of AI technology (together with other recent digital technology advancements) spilling over beyond the mega-cap winners (such as the Mag 7) to the broader economy. The equity market has largely been dominated by a winner-takes-all dynamic in my mind, with a few companies leading the renovation and reaping most of the gains. But current market conditions lead me to see larger and larger odds that the benefits from technological renovations are spreading from those who pioneered the development also to those who are end users - which are the majority of the economy and also the rest of the 493 members stocks in the S&P 500.

In particular, I expect the normalization of interest rates to continue and the Federal Reserve to continue easing its fund rates. Such an easing, of course, will help all companies, both those who develop AI (and other advanced digital tools) and those who use them. But my thesis is that falling borrowing costs tend to benefit capital-intensive value sectors more than capital-light sectors such as the tech sector. Also, as argued in a recent article, judging by the latest nonfarm productivity data, I believe AI technologies are transitioning into broad-based implementation, and I expect this to benefit traditional industries such as healthcare, manufacturing, etc. But obviously, this outlook entails uncertainties such as how the Fed changes its dot plot and also how the hyperscalers build out their digital infrastructure to support expanding deployment.

Before wrapping up, let's also take a quick look at the technical chart. The chart below shows the price ratio of VOOV and VOO, as another measure to illustrate the relative valuation dynamics between value and SP500. The chart shows the formation of an upward wedge pattern as shown by the dotted blue lines. The pattern for SCHD and VOO is very similar. For VOOV and VOO, their price ratio has been forming higher lows but capped to a resistance of around 0.308 since last October. As the wedge narrows, the odds of a decisive breakout increase in my experience.

Of course, the breakout can occur in either direction. But for the reasons analyzed above, I see larger odds for an upward breakout than a downward breakout. To close, my goals in this article are twofold: A) to argue that a rotation towards value is undergoing, and B) to argue why the rotation has more room to go. With value stocks and funds still trading at discounts to the S&P 500 by historical standards, I keep seeing a wider safety margin in value stocks and funds than in the S&P 500 (or other similar indices such as the DJI and NDX).

data.title: Novartis: Upside Increasingly Capped, 2026 Will Be A Transition Year (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
We have consistently maintained a supportive view on Novartis (NVS) (NVSEF), repeatedly highlighting upside in our analysis, which has been validated by the company’s successive upgrades to guidance. Here at The Lab, it was clear that "following the spin-off of its eye care business (Alcon) in April 2019, and its generics unit (Sandoz) in October 2023, Novartis is a pure-play innovative medicines business focused on oncology, immunology, neuroscience, and cardiovascular conditions." The company valuation and margins were set to increase. This was combined with solid execution and the right capital allocation priorities: 1) acquisition and 2) a $10 billion share buyback (Fig. 1), maintaining its dividend payment and a solid balance sheet. Given the recent positive stock price performance, we believe Novartis is fairly priced. On a numbers basis, the company reached our target price, so we moved our rating to neutral.

Fig. 1

Why are we now neutral?

We do not view Novartis as a poor investment; however, valuation remains a critical consideration. Before turning to the group's value, several points are worth highlighting:

Starting at the macro level, after the sharp share rally across EU pharma and Novartis, we are more constructive on sector fundamentals. Even if we see less risk around US drug pricing and the Novartis CEO expects to eliminate US tariff exposure by mid‑2026, we believe this optimism is broadly priced in. Here at the Lab, we believe there might be a sales deceleration in 2026, as reported in our January 2025 analysis (Strong Execution With A P/E Discount), we are less concerned about the medium-term top-line sales target. There are multiple patent expiries, which, over time, will be partially offset by the medium-term ramp of newer assets such as Pluvicto and Rhapsido. Still, these new drugs will carry execution risks.

We expect Kisqali and Scemblix to maintain strong momentum into Q4 2025 (Fig. 2). However, this quarter will mark higher generic entry competition. This is scheduled for Entresto, Tasigna, and Promacta. Novartis also disclosed this issue, as shown in Figure 3. Thus, we model faster volume and pricing erosion as these products transition deeper into the multi-source phase. So, for Q4, we see top-line sales of just above $16 billion. In Q4, gross-to-net adjustments in the US market will also boost sales growth. This dynamic is also captured in our 2026 estimates. On a positive note, we will be looking for the first read-throughs on the Rhapsido, though near-term revenue should be modest given heavy US sampling ahead of broader insurance coverage building in H2 2026. Related to the Q3 results, US Kesimpta sales were not mirrored in prescription trends, which we interpret as channel stocking. So, looking at Q4, we assume a destock and a return to more normal underlying demand.

Source: Novartis Q3 results ppt - Fig. 2

Fig. 3

At this stage, Novartis’ valuation looks demanding. Here at the Lab, we believe Wall Street has fully priced the company’s capital-allocation story (Fig. 4): potential M&A, a $10 billion buyback, and a growing DPS. This is combined with incremental US CAPEX ($23 billion), and collectively, this cap has near-term upside, leaving limited scope for further gains in capital efficiency.

Fig. 4

Earnings Change and Valuation

The company is due to report its Q4 2024 results on 4 February, but we prefer to wait and see. Despite highlighting risks at Novartis, we still see positive expectations for the fiscal year 2026. Here at the Lab, accounting for generic erosion, we see Novartis growing in both sales and core operating income to low single digits, with roughly a 1 basis-point margin drag from the recent Avidity Biosciences acquisition. We believe this is a phasing year with more risks in H1, followed by a likely rebound in H2. So, in numbers, considering the above, we see Novartis 2026 sales and EBIT at $58.4 billion and $22.1 billion, respectively. This compares with the 2025 full-year estimates of $57.1 billion in sales and $22 billion in core operating profit. Still looking at 2026, with an interest expense of $1.35 billion and a corporate tax rate of 16.5% (aligned with its historical tax rate), we derive a net profit of $17.23 billion. With the current outstanding shares, our EPS reached $8.2. Even with a $3 billion annual buyback, our EPS increased to $8.3. Continuing to apply a target P/E ratio of 14.5x, our Novartis target price reached $120.35. Even using an FWD sector median P/E of 19.5x (Fig. 5), Novartis's target price would be $161.85, and given the current stock price of $147 per share, we would take a neutral stance.

Fig. 5

Risks

Since initiating coverage in 2022, we have highlighted the following key risks: faster-than-expected generic erosion, adverse FX movements, weaker sales momentum from IRA-related pressures, and commercial/pipeline setbacks. We believe some of these risks will materialize in 2026. The company faces downside risks from new litigation and the ongoing dispute with Entresto. Novartis debt will likely increase, considering the company's current capital allocation priorities. The company faces additional regulatory requirements and CAPEX inflation on the new US manufacturing sites.

Conclusion

Higher generic competition and Kesimpta destocking activities will likely weigh on Q4 2025 results. We expect a transition year for Novartis, and the company's valuation is reasonably priced. For this reason, we moved our rating from Buy to Neutral.

data.title: The Fed Preview: A Defiantly Hawkish Pause
data.description:
The pause, but still a policy normalization

The Fed is set to meet for the January FOMC meeting on Wednesday, January 28th, and it is widely expected to leave the Federal Funds unchanged at a 3.5-3.75% range.

This is important as it will mark the end of the recent policy easing cycle - thus, practically, the Fed will be on a pause starting next week.

However, based on the Fed's Summary of Economic Projections from December, the Fed is still in a policy normalization mode.

Specifically, the Fed currently views that the long-term neutral rate is at 3%, meaning at this level, the policy rate is neither accommodative nor restrictive. Given the current level of the federal funds rate of 3.66%, the policy rate is still modestly restrictive.

The Fed seems to be concerned about the recent labor market weakness. Thus, it plans to preventively lower the Federal Funds rate to a neutral rate of 3%, which means two more cuts are expected.

At the same time, inflation is still elevated above the 2% target. Thus, the Fed plans to proceed carefully with further cuts, with one planned cut in 2026 and one in 2027.

Thus, practically, the Fed is entering a long pause, with two additional cuts expected over the next two years.

It's all about the estimated neutral rate

The estimated 3% neutral rate is basically a median of all individual FOMC members' projections. The central tendency measure of a neutral interest rate is 2.9% to 3.6%.

Thus, the current level of the federal funds rate is already at the top of the neutral rate range level, which means that policy normalization could be over, and no additional cuts are likely. This would be a hawkish view.

Alternatively, the Fed could cut three more times to the bottom of the neutral rate range of 2.9%, and still call it a policy normalization. This would be a dovish view.

The Fed independence concerns

The statutory Fed independence has been threatened lately, as it appears that the Trump administration is trying to take control of the FOMC.

Specifically, the Trump administration is calling for a much lower interest rate. The problem is that moving the federal funds rate to an accommodative level below 2.9% needs to be justified with a slowing growth, a weakening labor market, generally associated with a recession, and also cooling inflation.

The current conditions still don't warrant lowering the policy rate to an accommodative level.

The argument presented by the Fed Governor Miran is that the neutral rate is around 2%, well below the current consensus estimate of 3%. Thus, the policy normalization could continue until the Federal Funds rate reaches 2%, and Miran argues that this level should be reached quickly - meaning the Fed should be aggressively cutting interest rates to 2%.

Note, the Fed Governor Miran has been appointed by President Trump, and he seems to be the voice of the Administration at the FOMC.

Market expectations

The market seems to agree with the FOMC estimates of the neutral rate. Currently, the Federal Funds futures estimate the Federal Funds rate of 3.22% in December 2026 and 3.23% in December 2027. Thus, at this point, the market is actually in a process of pricing out one cut, where the consensus is one more cut in 2026, with around a 50% chance of another cut by the end of 2027.

Thus, the market is currently aligned with the FOMC projections and possibly pricing a slightly more hawkish policy.

The main risk

The primary risk is that the FOMC gets influenced by the Trump administration and lowers the estimate for the neutral rate - this could trigger an inflationary shock.

The reported inflation data, CPI and PPI numbers, have been noisy and thus might not represent true inflation. Thus, the Fed might not even consider these numbers to be detrimental for policy decisions.

The Fed's main inflationary concern is the objective of keeping the long-term inflationary expectations well anchored - and that's a market measure.

Specifically, the Fed needs to keep the 10Y breakeven inflation below the 2.5% level. Once the 10Y BE crosses the 2.5% level towards the 3% level, the long-term inflationary expectations de-anchor - this is when investors start questioning the credibility of the Fed's longer-term 2% inflation target.

Note, the Fed was forced to tighten monetary policy in 2022 when the 10Y BE spiked above 2.5% and reached 3%. If this happens again, the Fed will be forced to tighten as well. However, if the Fed is unable to hike in this situation due to the "administrative restrictions", the 10Y yields (US10Y) could spike and the USD (DXY) could collapse - that's the main risk given the current situation.

Based on the chart below, we are not in this situation yet, with the 10Y BE at 2.32%. However, the 10BE has been rising lately, and this is the trend to follow. At this time, the market still believes that the Fed will remain independent. The new Fed Chair announcement could change that perception, if the new Fed Chair is not deemed credible.

Implications

The Fed is currently in a policy normalization mode, with two additional cuts expected over the next two years, although the market is starting to price one cut less.

At this moment, inflation is not a problem, with long term inflationary expectations well anchored, although recently rising.

In addition, the market does not seem to be concerned about a recession, which is supported by a stable unemployment rate at 4.4% and low levels of initial claims for unemployment.

Finally, the credit spreads are very tight, with the BBB-10Y spread at 1.59%, meaning the credit risk is very low.

Thus, the current macro environment is very benign, which is supportive of a rising stock market (SP500), especially a rotation into the cyclicals and small caps (IWM).

However, the issue of the Fed's credibility is the major risk for the market. For this reason, the FOMC is likely to appear more hawkish and defiant, by emphasizing a long pause, especially at the post meeting FOMC press conference.

The current macro situation is likely short-lived, as the factors that have caused the recent labor market weakness are structural and policy-based; thus, the Fed's preventive policy actions might not help.

I expect further labor market weakness due to 1) a strict immigration policy, 2) tariffs, and 3) AI adoption. The Fed cannot counter these factors. Thus, I expect a significant economic slowdown, and more aggressive Fed cuts in 2026. This is not priced in by the market. Further, as the AI bubble potentially bursts, the negative wealth effect is likely to curb consumption and further increase recessionary pressures.

data.title: EMF: Emerging Market Exposure With 8% Discount And Moderate Yield
data.description:
Introduction

Closed-end funds offer an attractive investment class that covers various asset classes and promises high distributions to income investors. They can also offer reasonable total returns if the distributions are reinvested, but generally lag the performance of the S&P 500. They are not for everyone but are particularly appealing to retirees and income investors. However, it will be best to diversify in 7 to 10 CEFs covering many different asset classes and market segments.

During the last decade, the S&P 500, or U.S.-based domestic funds, have outperformed almost every other country. However, that changed last year when most foreign markets, both developed and emerging markets, outperformed the S&P 500 for the first time in many years. It is highly likely that this trend will continue in 2026. This is mainly because the U.S. market had become highly overvalued in comparison to the rest of the world, and now the rest of the world is catching up.

So, in this backdrop, we think the Templeton Emerging Markets Fund (EMF) is worth considering as it provides exposure to a large number of emerging economies/countries with one click of the mouse. However, we should allocate only a small portion of our portfolio to emerging markets.

Fund's Background

The fund EMF was launched in February 1987 as a perpetual equity closed-end fund. So, the fund has been around for a long time, almost 39 years. This fund provides exposure to a host of emerging markets, though China and Taiwan together represent nearly 40% of the assets. The fund's investment objective is to provide long-term capital appreciation by investing, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its net assets in emerging country equity securities.

The TTM (12-month trailing) yield of the fund was 8.87%; however, the yield without the special distribution at the year-end comes to around 4.75%. The current discount is decent at -8%.

As per the Fund's literature,

The Fund seeks long-term capital appreciation by investing, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its net assets in emerging country equity securities. Our investment strategy employs a fundamental, value-oriented, long-term approach. We focus on the market price of a company's securities relative to our evaluation of the company's long-term earnings, asset value and cash flow potential. As we look for investments, we focus on specific companies and undertake in-depth research to construct an action list from which we make our buy decisions. Before we make a purchase, we look at the company's potential for earnings and growth over a five-year horizon. During our analysis, we also consider the company's position in its sector, the economic framework and political environment.

Our investment strategy employs a bottom-up, research-driven approach focused on identifying long-term earnings power at a discount to intrinsic value. Our portfolio construction process seeks to build a research-driven, high-conviction portfolio that is primarily driven by company specific factors and focused on the long term.

Other Salient Features of This Fund Are as Follows:

  • The fund is managed by the Franklin Templeton group. It features among the world's largest asset managers, serving clients in more than 150 countries.
  • The fund uses almost zero or insignificant leverage. As of 12/31/2025, it had only about 1.75% of fixed-rate loans.
  • The fund is an actively managed fund and has an expense ratio of 1.34%.
  • Since its inception until the end of Dec. 2025, EMF has paid a total distribution of $48.32 a share. The NAV at inception was $20, and as of Jan. 20, 2026, it was $20.08.
  • Since its inception nearly 39 years ago, from Jan. 1988 until Dec. 2025, the fund has returned 11.98% on an annualized basis (NAV, including distributions), compared to 11.31% for the S&P 500. However, please note that this performance has been provided with frequent drawdowns and a higher volatility than the S&P 500.
  • As of 12/31/2025, the fund had 85 holdings. As such, the fund is well diversified amongst sectors and various emerging markets. However, it has additional risks as it has no exposure to the developed markets or the U.S. market. Also, China and Taiwan together have nearly 40% exposure.
  • As of Dec. 31, 2025, the fund had roughly $286 million in total assets, with roughly $281 million in net assets.
  • As of Jan. 20, 2026, EMF's distribution yield based on the regular quarterly distribution amount is only 4.73%. However, it usually pays a larger amount at the year's end. On the TTM (12-month trailing) basis, the yield was nearly 9%.

Financial Outlook

Let's look at the Fund's financial health and performance. The most recent annual report that the fund has released is for the period from Sep. 2024 to Aug. 31, 2025, and the previous annual report is for the period ending Aug. 2024.

The net investment income (or NII in short) is the net income that a fund earns from its investments in the form of dividends, distributions, interests, and any other generated income (for example, buy-write options) minus all of the fund's expenses, including management fees, operating expenses, commissions, and interest on leverage, if any. For equity-based funds, especially in high-growth sectors like technology, the NII is not very relevant compared to fixed-income funds.

Here is what it looks like in terms of NII, Distributions, and Net Assets at the beginning and end of the statement period.

(all amounts are in US$ (except Shares Outstanding) for the 12-month period ending Aug. 31, 2025, and the 12-month prior period ending Aug. 31, 2024; negative amounts are shown inside parentheses).

Table-1A:

Table-1B: Per Share Data

Distributions

The fund has a managed distribution policy, and since the beginning of 2025, it has provided a fixed amount of $0.22 every quarter. However, at the end of December 2025, it also provided a higher distribution amount totaling $0.996, resulting in an 8.87% yield on a TTM (12-month trailing) basis. However, based purely on the fixed distribution amount of $0.22 a quarter, the yield works out to be only 4.73%.

Since its inception in Feb. 1987 until the end of Dec. 2025, EMF has paid a total distribution of $48.32 a share. The NAV at inception was $20, and as of Jan. 20, 2026, it is $20.08.

Since its inception nearly 39 years ago (from Mar. 1987 to Dec. 2025), the fund has provided total returns (including distributions) at an annualized rate of 11.15% on NAV. This is pretty good and exceeded the returns of the S&P 500 at 10.72%.

So, is the Distribution Covered?

EMF follows a managed distribution policy and, since Jan. 2025, pays a fixed amount on a quarterly basis. All of the fund's assets are invested in equities of emerging markets. There is some dividend income, but half of it is consumed by the expenses, including the management fee. So, the fund has to depend to the extent of 75% (or more) on the capital gains. For a fund like this, what is important is that NAV is either stable or preferably growing over 5-year and 10-year periods. The current NAV of $20.30 is at similar levels to what it was at the end of 2021.

The table below shows that none of the distributions in the last five years were paid as ROC (return of capital). Also, the overall return of the fund since inception is respectable at almost 11%. So, we can safely conclude that the distribution is highly sustainable.

Table-2:

Discount/Premium

The fund is currently trading at a decent discount of -7.42% (as of 01/20/2026), which is slightly inferior to its long-term averages. The 3-year and 5-year averages are -13.4% and -12.6%. This is also reflected in the Z-score values (shown on the right side in the charts below).

However, discount/premium is only one of those factors amongst many that determine the valuation.

Chart-3A: Premium/Discount Information (Past 1 Year)

Chart-3B: Premium/Discount Information (Past 5 Years)

Fund's Holdings

The fund was invested in 85 securities as of Dec. 31, 2025. The largest five sectors were Technology, Consumer Discretionary, Financials, Industrials, and Communications. The fund is a bit concentrated in the top holdings, as the top 10 positions represented nearly 49% of the total assets, while the largest holding, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), represented 15% of the total.

Performance

Here is the performance of the fund, EMF, as presented on the fund's website.

Table-5: Performance as of Dec. 31, 2025

We can see in the above table that the long-term performance of the EMF fund is superior to other similar funds, especially when you compare it with the ETF. On a long-term basis, it is comparable to the S&P 500 as well, but with much higher volatility and drawdowns. The year 2025 turned out to be the year to reverse the trend, and most emerging markets performed better than the U.S. markets. Most observers believe this trend will continue in 2026.

Risk Factors

Investors need to be aware of certain risk factors that are associated with this fund and CEFs in general. Risk factors could be summarized as follows:

International investment risks: In addition to general equity investment risks, investment in International markets is subject to special risks, including country-specific risks, currency fluctuations, and social, economic, and political uncertainties. This can also cause a heightened level of volatility. These risks can be even more magnified in the case of emerging markets, as their economies and market depth can be smaller compared to developed markets.

Country-specific risks: The fund is invested in many different countries from the emerging markets, some of which represent significant allocations. Any adverse political or economic events in a country could weigh down the fund. In addition, certain foreign securities may not be as liquid as U.S. securities.

Frontier-market countries risks: Some of the countries in which the fund invests can be classified as frontier markets. These are the countries that generally have smaller economies, less stable governments, and/or less developed capital markets. As a result, such investments can result in higher price volatility and, in some extreme instances, result in illiquidity of assets.

Leverage risk: This fund usually employs a very low or insignificant amount of leverage. It employs leverage through the use of a revolving credit facility. Generally, leverage can work in both ways. It can provide higher returns in good times but can magnify the adverse impacts during downturns.

Market risks: Almost all of the fund's assets are invested in equities, and besides company- and country-specific risks, it is exposed to market-related risks. If the world economy (or any major economy, such as the U.S.) enters into a recession, that can impact most of the markets. The market is unpredictable and can have positive or negative swings based on a variety of factors. Also, markets can be impacted by geopolitical situations.

Concluding Thoughts

The conventional wisdom says that the international markets (both developed and emerging) are likely to outperform the US markets in the coming few years, mainly due to valuations. This was the case in the year 2025, when the international markets outshone the S&P 500 after a long period of underperformance. Though there is no guarantee that this will happen, the odds are quite high. As we can see in the performance table, EMF provided a massive 58% return in 2025, compared to less than 18% for the S&P 500. In this backdrop, it is always advisable to keep your portfolio properly diversified amongst various markets and asset classes. Amongst other emerging market funds, and especially the iShares emerging market ETF, the EMF fund has provided superior performance over the years.

The regular yield is under 5%, but with year-end specials, most years, it can be much higher.

We rate EMF as a 'hold' for the existing owners. However, if you are a new buyer, you should plan to buy it in multiple lots, taking advantage of dollar-cost-averaging.

data.title: This One Macro Shift Is Quietly Reshaping My Entire Portfolio
data.description:
The stock market (SPY) is currently very overvalued based on most valuation metrics, and I have recently warned that it is getting quite risky. However, perhaps an even bigger risk to portfolios than an overvalued market is that paper assets are currently getting vastly outpaced by commodities (GSG), and in particular precious metals like silver (SLV) and gold (GLD), which have historically proved to be durable stores of value and monetary mediums of exchange, whereas fiat currencies come and go. Moreover, the way economic and geopolitical conditions are lining up indicates that paper money-denominated assets could remain high-risk and major underperformers moving forward. In this article, I will detail why and share how I am positioning my portfolio as a result.

Inflation, Rates, and the Setup Few Investors Are Pricing In

Inflation remains persistently high, with CPI inflation in December 2025 coming in at 2.7% year-over-year and the core CPI coming in at 2.6% year-over-year. Even the Fed’s preferred inflation metric, the PCE price index, came in at 2.8% year-over-year in November, on both a headline and core basis.

This indicates that inflation, while perhaps not as bad as some feared in the wake of tariffs, is still well above the Fed’s target rate.

Meanwhile, GDP has been running hot with a 4.4% annualized growth rate in Q3. With significant AI and factory-related CAPEX kicking into high gear this year, that should help push the economy into even faster growth. Meanwhile, the Fed has been cutting rates, and with Chairman Powell likely to be replaced soon by a more rate-cut-prone chairman by President Trump, there is a reasonable chance that the Fed will continue cutting rates this year as well. If this pushes interest rates lower while the economy is running hot and inflation is already showing to be persistently high relative to the Fed’s target 2% rate, we could very well see a resurgence in inflation.

The De-Dollarization Trend Nobody Can Ignore

These factors, coupled with the accelerating de-dollarization trend around the world, fueled by runaway fiscal deficits in the U.S. as well as changing perceptions of the United States' role in the world and how it relates to other countries, are further driving up the value of real assets/reducing the value of the dollar.

This is because institutions and central banks, as well as many investors, are increasingly diversifying out of U.S. Treasuries (TLT) and other dollar assets, instead pushing capital into real assets like precious metals, real estate (VNQ), and infrastructure (UTF).

Why Real Assets Still Look Cheap

Perhaps making this play even more compelling is that while gold and silver have been on tremendous runs recently, many other real asset stocks remain quite attractively priced. For example, many publicly traded REITs trade at significant discounts to net asset value, and even leading blue-chip REITs like Realty Income (O) trade at a modest premium to NAV while still offering a very attractive dividend yield north of 5%. Meanwhile, blue-chip multi-family apartment REITs like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) and Camden Property Trust (CPT), as well as blue-chip timberland REIT Weyerhaeuser (WY), trade at material discounts to NAV, as do single-family residential REITs like Invitation Homes (INVH) and American Homes for Rent (AMH).

Meanwhile, on the infrastructure side, numerous midstream MLPs offer sky-high yields backed by very strong balance sheets and inflation-beating distribution growth rates, as evidenced by the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) offering over 8% yield and the likes of Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and others offering yields in the high single digits. Additionally, you can buy AI-related infrastructure names like Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC) and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) at 5%+ distribution yields backed by double-digit growth rates and BBB+ credit ratings.

Finally, while I do think that gold and silver are no longer nearly as cheap as they were a few years ago, when I was pounding the table to load up on them, I still have a very large position in both. I hold them primarily through bullion leases that earn me an attractive yield while I hold my bullion as part of a long-term thesis that the dollar will continue to devalue and gold and silver will become increasingly prized as a more durable store of value and medium of exchange.

Investor Takeaway

With the market looking so overhyped right now, and leading investors warning that we are likely in a bubble and could even be in for a lost decade, as well as the fact that inflationary pressures remain high and the de-dollarization trend appears to be accelerating, I am finding increasing appeal in real asset-focused income investments, especially when I can buy them at attractive valuations. As a result, I am increasingly allocating capital towards REITs and infrastructure, wherever I find that value and quality intersect, while also continuing to hold my very large bullion lease positions in gold and silver, as I continue to seek long-term outperformance in my 7–8% yielding, low beta, and market-beating portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: This Is How You Value Tesla
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has slightly lagged the broader market index since my last article on the stock:

Thesis

Tesla is due to release its Q4 FY25 results on Wednesday, 28 January. Here's how I'm looking at the stock ahead of this earnings release:

  1. Tesla is making promising progress on scaling robotaxis.
  2. Favorable revenue mix shifts allow for gross margin expansion.
  3. Believe it or not, the valuations are reasonable.
  4. TSLA/S&P 500 (SP500) may be ready to break out to the upside.

Tesla is Making Promising Progress on Scaling Robotaxis

In the last quarter's earnings call, Musk stated that they expect robotaxis to be running without the supervision of safety drivers by the end of 2025:

But we are expecting to have no safety drivers in at least large parts of Austin by the end of this year. So within a few months, we expect to have no safety drivers at all at least in parts of Austin.

- CEO Elon Musk in the Q3 FY25 earnings call.

As Musk has a tendency to overpromise and deliver late, I believe it is a big success for them to hit this milestone just 1 month later than forecast. Unsupervised robotaxis for Tesla is a major validation of the company's cameras-only approach. This strategic choice makes production more cost-efficient and scalable than Alphabet's (GOOGL, GOOG) Waymo, which relies on expensive LIDAR technology.

In the upcoming earnings call on January 28th, I would be keen to hear updates showcasing continued momentum in the robotaxis rollout. In my opinion, this is the single biggest narrative driving the value of TSLA stock right now. Notice that some long-time Tesla bulls are banking on adoption rates surprising to the upside:

We’re keeping an eye on how quickly Tesla’s footprint is expanding here, and it’s expanding a lot faster, I think, than many people expected... So I think this is actually going to happen faster than most analysts think, in terms of the proliferation of robotaxis, especially if we get more federal legislation instead of just state by state.

- Ark Invest CEO Cathie Wood.

Favorable Revenue Mix Shifts Allow for Gross Margin Expansion

The robotaxis business utilizes software to monetize a physical asset multiple times. Hence, as the robotaxi ramp scales up and FSD subscriptions grow, I believe there is a strong case for the company's traditional automotive gross margin-focused profile of sub-20% to something closer to 40-50% over the longer term (still lower than the typical 70% or 80% in software businesses due to the hardware aspect of the robotaxi vehicles):

Believe It or Not, the Valuations Are Reasonable

Ignore TSLA's 231x 1-yr fwd P/E in the chart below. What we must focus on is the range of multiples for its comps:

Let's do a reverse DCF (I call it reverse DCF because in form and function it is one, but instead of cash flows, I'm using earnings as the key driver) for Tesla to find out exactly what the market is implying about the stock's growth expectations. This is the implied growth CAGR formula:

I'll take a risk-free rate of 4.2% and an equity risk premium of 4.4%. That gets us an implied U.S. market return of 8.5%. Now let's respect CEO Elon Musk's track record in continuously building large new businesses in new industries by assuming a higher steady-state P/E multiple of 35x. I think that is reasonable, as it is just mildly above other mega tech businesses' P/E multiples in the low 30x range. And for the time horizon till the steady state, I'll assume 15 years. I think this is long enough to let a lot of the economics of the robotaxi business and some part of the humanoid potential to materialize. With these assumptions, we get an implied 15-year earnings CAGR of 24%:

I think it's easier to think about this from the perspective of the final TTM earnings run rate 15 years from now in 2040. The calculation above says that's $148 billion. So what this means is that for TSLA stock to merely earn a market-level return over the next 15 years, the company needs to have a 35x P/E multiple in 2040 with an annual earnings base of $148 billion.

Is This Reasonable?

I'll break it down by digging into the earnings potential for each of Tesla's 3 big bets: the robotaxis business, general-purpose humanoid robots, and the existing automotive and energy businesses:

Robotaxis

Some industry estimates peg the global robotaxi market to be worth $1.4 trillion by 2040, with Tesla taking around 35% market share. I'll be a bit more conservative and assume 25% market share at the same total market size. That gets us annual revenues of $350 billion coming from robotaxis for Tesla in 2040. Now for the net profit margins, I'm assuming 25%, as it seems reasonable for a software-driven physical service product. For context, Ark Invest estimates 32% EBITDA margins for Tesla's robotaxi business. Ultimately that gets us a robotaxi annual PAT contribution in 2040 of $88 billion.

Humanoid robots

The general-purpose robotics market (which humanoid robots fall under) is expected to be worth $370 billion by 2040. Given its early leadership with the Optimus robot and Tesla's deep, pioneering manufacturing expertise in EVs, I believe 25% market share by 2040 is a reasonable estimate. This gets us an annual revenue base of $93 billion. As humanoid robots are also a software-driven physical product like robotaxis, I will assume the same 25% PAT margins. So overall, Tesla's humanoid robot PAT contribution in 2040 comes to $23 billion.

Existing automotive and energy businesses

I'm taking a 15% earnings CAGR for these businesses on account of the fact that the EV and clean energy transitions are still major long-term growth themes today. Applying this to the current base of $5.9 billion PAT gets us a $48 billion PAT contribution by 2040.

Putting all this together, we get a total 2040 PAT estimate for Tesla of $159 billion, which is a little bit (7.2%) higher than the $148 billion implied by the reverse DCF analysis:

So looking at it in this reverse DCF plus granular business potential estimates way, I think TSLA stock's valuations seem reasonable, but the limited upside indicates a low margin of safety for adopting a bullish view.

TSLA/SPX500 May Be Ready to Break Out to the Upside

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of TSLA vs. SPX500

The relative monthly charts of TSLA vs. SPX500 show us a sequence of higher lows, which I interpret as a sign of desperation and enthusiasm by the buyers to bid the ratio prices up. The low volatility hold setup supported by a clean, bullish engulfing candle before the monthly resistance level also looks like a favorable setup for an impending breakout to the upside.

Takeaway & Positioning

My analysis of Tesla has increasingly focused more on the robotaxis business potential and the humanoid robot growth optionality and less on the ebbs and flows of the existing automotive business. It didn't used to be this way. But nowadays, I truly believe the Tesla story hinges upon the success or failure of the company's new growth bets. And looking at TSLA stock through this lens, my valuation analysis suggests close to fair pricing. This may seem shocking to investors who only look at the >200x 1-yr fwd P/E. But as I have stated before in coverage of other high-growth names such as Palantir (PLTR) and Rocket Lab (RKLB), you cannot analyze these kinds of stocks looking at a short-term 1-yr fwd P/E.

From a business perspective, it is good to see Tesla rapidly making progress on scaling robotaxis, unlocking milestones such as unsupervised self-driving in Austin, Texas. I think further updates on the rollout will be the most important thing to focus on in the upcoming earnings call on Wednesday.

The charts signal a bullish possibility as the ratio prices on TSLA vs. SPX500 may be ready to break out to the upside.

Overall, I rate the stock as a "Market Perform/Hold" despite a bullish fundamental stance due to a lack of margin of safety in the valuations.

How to Interpret Hunting Alpha's Ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.
